From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Firearm component that houses the operating parts of the weapon

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Disassembled_mauser_long_action.jpg/250px-Disassembled_mauser_long_action.jpg)](/wiki/File:Disassembled_mauser_long_action.jpg)

A disassembled [Gewehr 98](/wiki/Gewehr_98 "Gewehr 98") action showing a partially disassembled receiver and bolt

In [firearms terminology](/wiki/Firearms_terminology "Firearms terminology") and law, the **firearm frame** or **receiver** is the part of a [firearm](/wiki/Firearm "Firearm") which integrates other components by providing [housing](/wiki/Housing_(engineering) "Housing (engineering)") for internal [action](/wiki/Action_(firearms) "Action (firearms)") components such as the [hammer](/wiki/Hammer_(firearms) "Hammer (firearms)"), [bolt](/wiki/Bolt_(firearms) "Bolt (firearms)") or [breechblock](/wiki/Breechblock "Breechblock"), [firing pin](/wiki/Firing_pin "Firing pin") and [extractor](/wiki/Extractor_(firearms) "Extractor (firearms)"), and has [threaded](/wiki/Screw_thread "Screw thread") interfaces for externally attaching ("receiving") components such as the [barrel](/wiki/Gun_barrel "Gun barrel"), [stock](/wiki/Stock_(firearms) "Stock (firearms)"), [trigger mechanism](/wiki/Trigger_(firearms) "Trigger (firearms)") and [iron](/wiki/Iron_sights "Iron sights")/[optical sights](/wiki/Sight_(device)#Optical_sights "Sight (device)").[[1]](#cite_note-Receiver_definition-1) Some firearm designs, such as the [AR-15](/wiki/AR-15 "AR-15") platform, feature receivers that have 2 separate sub-assemblies called the **upper receiver** which houses the barrel/[trunnion](/wiki/Trunnion "Trunnion"), bolt components etc and the **lower receiver** (**trigger mechanism housing** in some cases) that holds the fire control group, pistol grip, selector, stock etc.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The receiver is often made of [forged](/wiki/Forge "Forge"), machined, or stamped [steel](/wiki/Steel "Steel") or [aluminium](/wiki/Aluminium "Aluminium"). Apart from these traditional materials, modern techniques have introduced [polymer](/wiki/Polymer "Polymer") and [sintered](/wiki/Sintering "Sintering") metal powder receivers to the market.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Mounting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Receiver_(firearms)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Mounting")]

A barrel may typically be affixed to a firearm receiver using barrel and receiver [action threads](/wiki/Action_threads "Action threads") or similar methods.

## In Canadian law

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Receiver_(firearms)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: In Canadian law")]

See also: [Firearms regulation in Canada § Classifications](/wiki/Firearms_regulation_in_Canada#Classifications "Firearms regulation in Canada")

The receiver or frame is legally the firearm, and as such it is the part that requires a manufacturer's [serial number](/wiki/Serial_number "Serial number") and valid [Possession and Acquisition Licence](/wiki/Possession_and_Acquisition_Licence "Possession and Acquisition Licence") to acquire and own. In the case of a [handgun frame or revolver frame](/wiki/Firearms_regulation_in_Canada#Restricted "Firearms regulation in Canada"), it is the part that requires a Restricted-class [PAL](/wiki/Possession_and_Acquisition_Licence "Possession and Acquisition Licence") (RPAL), and [registration](/wiki/Canadian_Firearms_Registry "Canadian Firearms Registry").

## In US law

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Receiver_(firearms)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: In US law")]

See also: [Parts kit](/wiki/Parts_kit "Parts kit")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Stag2wi.jpg/250px-Stag2wi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stag2wi.jpg)

[AR-15](/wiki/AR-15 "AR-15") rifles showing their configurations with different upper receivers. The lower receiver is visible at the bottom

For the purposes of [United States law](/wiki/Gun_law_in_the_United_States "Gun law in the United States"), the receiver or frame is legally the firearm,[[4]](#cite_note-4) and as such it is the controlled part. The definition of which assembly is the legal receiver varies from firearm to firearm, under US law. Generally, the law requires licensed manufacturers and importers to mark the designated receiver with a [serial number](/wiki/Serial_number "Serial number"), the manufacturer or importer, the model and caliber. Makers of receivers are restricted by [International Traffic in Arms Regulations](/wiki/International_Traffic_in_Arms_Regulations "International Traffic in Arms Regulations"). Thus, in the case of a firearm that has multiple receiver parts, such as the [AR-15](/wiki/AR-15 "AR-15"), which has an upper and a lower receiver, the legally controlled part is the one that is serialized.[[5]](#cite_note-GD2015-5)

For the AR-15 rifle, the lower receiver assembly is legally considered the actual receiver,[[5]](#cite_note-GD2015-5) although it is functionally a [chassis](/wiki/Chassis#Firearms "Chassis") that also houses the separate [trigger](/wiki/Trigger_(firearms) "Trigger (firearms)") group. In the [FN-FAL](/wiki/FN-FAL "FN-FAL") rifle, it is the upper assembly that is serialized and legally considered the receiver.[[6]](#cite_note-Jenzen-JonesSpleeters2015-6)[[7]](#cite_note-ATF_Rul._2008-1-7) This has led to prosecutors dropping charges against illegal manufacturing of AR-type firearms to avoid court precedents establishing that neither the upper nor the lower receiver individually contain all the components to be legally classified as a firearm.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Unfinished receivers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Receiver_(firearms)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Unfinished receivers")]

"Unfinished receivers", also called "80 percent receivers", "80% lowers", or "blanks", are partially completed receivers with no serial numbers. Purchasers must perform their own finishing work in order to make the receiver usable. The finishing of receivers for sale or distribution by unlicensed persons is against US law.[[9]](#cite_note-Horwitz-9) Because an unfinished 80% receiver is not a firearm, purchasers do not need to pass a background check.[[9]](#cite_note-Horwitz-9) The resulting firearm is known as a "[ghost gun](/wiki/Ghost_gun "Ghost gun")".[[10]](#cite_note-Stanton-10)

During the [Biden Administration](/wiki/Biden_Administration "Biden Administration"), the [ATF](/wiki/ATF "ATF") imposed regulations on the sale and marketing of unfinished receivers and kits containing them by revising the legal definition of receivers to include "a partially complete, disassembled, or nonfunctional frame or receiver, including a frame or receiver parts kit, that is designed to or may readily be completed, assembled, restored, or otherwise converted to function as a frame or receiver". (The term "receiver" was also redefined as referring to non-handgun firearms, while "frame" was redefined as referring to handguns exclusively.) The new definitions went into effect on August 24, 2022.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In the case [VanDerStok v. Garland](/wiki/VanDerStok_v._Garland "VanDerStok v. Garland"), filed on June 30, 2023, a federal judge in the [United States District Court for the Northern District of Texas](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Northern_District_of_Texas "United States District Court for the Northern District of Texas") ruled that the new ATF regulations exceeded the agency's statutory authority, and struck them down.[[12]](#cite_note-12) However, on August 8, 2023, the Texas court's nationwide [vacatur](/wiki/Vacatur "Vacatur") was temporarily placed on hold by the [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States"), leaving the new ATF regulations on unfinished receivers in place.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The case was appealed to the [United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Fifth_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit") in November of 2023, with a three-judge panel upholding the District Court's ruling that the ATF had exceeded their statutory authority, striking the rule down; although the Supreme Court once again issued an emergency order allowing the ATF to continue enforcing the regulation while the litigation proceeded. On April 22, 2024 the case of [Bondi v. VanDerStok](/wiki/Bondi_v._VanDerStok "Bondi v. VanDerStok") was accepted on appeal to the Supreme Court of the United States, and on March 26, 2025 in a 7-2 decision,[[14]](#cite_note-14) the court upheld the Biden-era regulation, holding that "weapons parts kits are firearms within the scope of the [Gun Control Act](/wiki/Gun_Control_Act_of_1968 "Gun Control Act of 1968")" and affirmed the government’s authority to regulate them as such. This vacated the Fifth Circuit's ruling, allowing the ATF rule to remain in place.[[15]](#cite_note-15) However, the Court's ruling did not outlaw all unfinished frames and receivers that are unserialized, it only applies to "certain partially complete frames or receivers sold in weapon part kits, which include everything needed to quickly assemble a working handgun", therefore the ruling primarily applies to 80% pistol frames bundled in kits. Unfinished [AR lowers](/wiki/AR-15-style_rifle "AR-15-style rifle") are still legal under the Court's ruling, so long as they are sold individually and not as part of a kit with [jigs](/wiki/Jig_(tool) "Jig (tool)") and instructions, and 76% pistol frames are still legal.[[16]](#cite_note-16) As of March, 2026 unfinished AR lowers and 76 percent pistol frames are still being legally sold by companies like 80 Percent Arms and 80-Lowers.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

#### State Bans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Receiver_(firearms)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: State Bans")]

A number of states have passed laws making "ghost guns" illegal by banning the sale of unfinished firearm frames and receivers, and the possession of unserialized firearms. As of March 26, 2026 unfinished frames and receivers, aka "80 percent lowers", and unserialized "ghost guns", are illegal in the following United States jurisdictions:[[19]](#cite_note-19)

* [California](/wiki/Gun_laws_in_California "Gun laws in California")
* [Colorado](/wiki/Gun_laws_in_Colorado "Gun laws in Colorado")
* [Connecticut](/wiki/Gun_laws_in_Connecticut "Gun laws in Connecticut")
* [Delaware](/wiki/Gun_laws_in_Delaware "Gun laws in Delaware")
* [District of Columbia](/wiki/Gun_laws_in_the_District_of_Columbia "Gun laws in the District of Columbia")
* [Hawaii](/wiki/Gun_laws_in_Hawaii "Gun laws in Hawaii")
* [Illinois](/wiki/Gun_laws_in_Illinois "Gun laws in Illinois")
* [Maine](/wiki/Gun_laws_in_Maine "Gun laws in Maine")
* [Maryland](/wiki/Gun_laws_in_Maryland "Gun laws in Maryland")

## 3D printed receivers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Receiver_(firearms)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 3D printed receivers")]

As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Receiver_(firearms)&action=edit), several designs and at least two designs for [3D printable](/wiki/3D_printed_firearm "3D printed firearm") polymer lower unfinished receivers for the AR-15 have been released: the [AR Lower V5](/wiki/AR_Lower_V5 "AR Lower V5") and the [Charon](/wiki/Charon_(gun) "Charon (gun)"). 3D printed designs may also be used to produce [privately made firearms](/wiki/Privately_made_firearm "Privately made firearm").[[20]](#cite_note-Greenberg-20)
